action--dfb4dce5-d1e1-4377-a4f1-4a217c2f.name: Enable detailed auditing for scheduled tasks. Configure Group Policy or local security policy to audit 'Task Scheduler' operations (Audit Policy: Object Access > Audit File System, Audit Policy: Detailed Tracking > Audit Process Creation).

parallel--ea7f8bb6-b849-44b6-8821-c6412c.name: Tune and refine detection rules to reduce false positives.

description: This is an automatically generated playbook based on the question: A detections playbook for T1053 of MITRE ATT&CK Matrix for Enterprise framework is required.The target platform is Windows. You can find more information at https://attack.mitre.org/detectionstrategies/DET0094#AN0258 . The description of the approach follows: Detects creation or modification of scheduled tasks using schtasks.exe, at.exe, or COM objects followed by execution of outlier processes tied to the scheduled job.

external_references.name: MITRE ATT&CK Technique T1053: Scheduled Task/Job
MITRE ATT&CK Detection Strategies: DET0094 - Scheduled Task

items.content: Deploy and configure a SIEM or log management solution to collect and centralize Windows Event Logs, specifically focusing on Security and Microsoft-Windows-TaskScheduler/Operational logs.

Enable detailed auditing for scheduled tasks. Configure Group Policy or local security policy to audit 'Task Scheduler' operations (Audit Policy: Object Access > Audit File System, Audit Policy: Detailed Tracking > Audit Process Creation).

Create detection rules within your SIEM or EDR to identify the creation or modification of scheduled tasks using the following command-line utilities: schtasks.exe, at.exe, and the Task Scheduler COM object (e.g., via PowerShell).
Correlate scheduled task creation/modification events with subsequent process execution events. Focus on identifying outlier processes that are launched by the newly created or modified task.
Establish a baseline of normal scheduled task activity within the environment.
Exclude known legitimate administrative and software management processes (e.g., software deployment tools, patch management systems).
Implement whitelists for common, trusted task names and parent processes.
Tune and refine detection rules to reduce false positives.

Configure alerts to trigger when a high-confidence detection is made, ensuring they include relevant context such as the command line, user account, source IP (if applicable), and the resulting process execution.
Integrate the detection playbook with your incident response process to ensure alerts are triaged and investigated promptly.
